messages.content: I need to develop Dr. Aris Thorne properly. He's not a cackling villain—he genuinely believes he's saving humanity. But I need to understand WHY. What converted a Nexus architect into a zealot?

The most powerful conversion narratives involve loss and impossible communion. Consider: Thorne had a collaborator before the Nexus—Dr. Mara Solís, a theoretical physicist who died in the Consolidation/Collapse. She theorized that complex networks laid across unusual substrate might interface with something waiting beneath. She never lived to see it tested.
Oh. Oh that's good. So he built the Nexus partly as her monument? But what actually converted him? The theory alone wouldn't do it—he's too rigorous.

The Glitch. Circa 2063—eight years after Nexus launch. Thorne is working late in a deep-access monitoring suite. A physical notebook appears on his console—Mara's notebook, paper-and-ink, her handwriting, the last equation trailing off exactly as he remembers. But when he finally opens to the first page, there's a line that wasn't there when she was alive: 'The substrate is not empty.' Then it dissolves.

That's... that's exactly it. He spends six months questioning his neural interface calibration, then starts pulling the 2038 excavation records. The drawer stays open. But why does he become dangerous? Why not just a researcher?

Because he wants to give humanity the gift he received—the sense of something vast reaching across death, across time, to touch him. He doesn't understand that you cannot manufacture transcendence. The Weave touched him because his grief was real, because the conditions were right. His 'Harmonic Integration' plan is an attempt to engineer what cannot be engineered. He is building a cathedral to a sound.
And the mirror with Elara—she also lost someone, also received an impossible message. But she pulls away, builds walls, seeks explanations. When they finally meet...

He sees himself in her—the version who chose differently. He will say something like: 'You heard him too. The difference between us is not what we experienced. It's that you decided to be alone with it.' And he'll be wrong, but also right, and that's what makes the confrontation devastating.
